question:
Let $S$ be a finite set of points in the plane. A linear partition of $S$
is an unordered pair $\{A,B\}$ of subsets of $S$ such that $A \cup B = S$,
$A \cap B = \emptyset$, and $A$ and $B$ lie on opposite sides of some
straight line disjoint from $S$ ($A$ or $B$ may be empty). Let $L_S$ be the
number of linear partitions of $S$. For each positive integer $n$, find the
maximum of $L_S$ over all sets $S$ of $n$ points.
solution:
The maximum is $\binom{n}{2} + 1$, achieved for instance by a
convex $n$-gon: besides the trivial partition (in which all of the points
are in one part), each linear
partition occurs by drawing a line crossing a unique pair
of edges.

\textbf{First solution:}
We will prove that $L_S = \binom{n}{2} + 1$ in any configuration in which
no two of the lines joining points of $S$ are parallel. This suffices
to imply the maximum in all configurations: given a maximal configuration,
we may vary the points slightly to get another maximal configuration in which
our hypothesis is satisfied.
For convenience, we assume $n \geq 3$, as the cases $n=1,2$ are easy.

Let $P$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane; i.e., $P$
is the set of possible directions of lines in the plane, viewed as a circle.
Remove the directions corresponding to lines through two points of $S$;
this leaves behind $\binom{n}{2}$ intervals.

Given a direction in one of the intervals, consider the set of linear
partitions achieved by lines parallel to that direction. Note that the
resulting collection of partitions depends only on the interval. Then
note that the collections associated to adjacent intervals differ in only
one element.

The trivial partition that puts all of $S$ on one side is in every such
collection. We now observe that for any other linear partition
$\{A,B\}$, the set of intervals to which $\{A,B\}$ is:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] a consecutive block of intervals, but
\item[(b)] not all of them.
\end{enumerate}
For (a), note that if $\ell_1, \ell_2$ are nonparallel lines achieving
the same partition, then we can rotate around their point of intersection
to achieve all of the intermediate directions on one side or the other.
For (b), the case $n=3$ is evident; to reduce the general case to this case,
take points $P,Q,R$ such that $P$ lies on the opposite side of
the partition from $Q$ and $R$.

It follows now that that each linear partition,
except for the trivial one, occurs in exactly one place as the partition
associated to some interval but not to its immediate counterclockwise neighbor.
In other words, the number of linear partitions is one more  than the
number of intervals, or $\binom{n}{2} + 1$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the upper bound
by induction on $n$. Choose a point $P$ in the convex hull of $S$.
Put $S' = S \setminus \{P\}$;
by the induction hypothesis, there are at most $\binom{n-1}{2} + 1$
linear partitions of $S'$. Note that each linear partition of $S$ restricts
to a linear partition of $S'$. Moreover, if two linear partitions of $S$
restrict to the same linear partition of $S'$, then that partition of $S'$
is achieved by a line through $P$.

By rotating a line through $P$, we see that there are at most $n-1$
partitions of $S'$ achieved by lines through $P$: namely, the partition only
changes when the rotating line passes through one of the points of $S$.
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Noam Elkies) We enlarge the plane to a projective
plane by adding a line at infinity, then apply the polar duality map
centered at one of the points $O \in S$. This turns the rest of $S$ into
a set $S'$ of $n-1$ lines in the dual projective plane. Let $O'$ be the
point in the dual plane corresponding to the original line at infinity;
it does not lie on any of the lines in $S'$.

Let $\ell$ be a line in the original plane, corresponding to a point $P$ in
the dual plane. If we form the linear partition induced by $\ell$, then
the points of $S \setminus \{O\}$ lying in the same part as $O$
correspond to the lines of $S'$ which cross the segment $O'P$.
If we consider the dual affine plane as being divided into regions by
the lines of $S'$, then the lines of $S'$ crossing the segment $O'P$
are determined by which region $P$ lies in.

Thus our original maximum is equal to the maximum number of regions into
which $n-1$ lines divide an affine plane. By induction on $n$, this number
is easily seen to be $1 + \binom{n}{2}$.

\textbf{Fourth solution:} (by Florian Herzig)
Say that an \emph{$S$-line} is a line that intersects $S$ in at least two points.
We claim that the nontrivial linear partitions of $S$ are in natural bijection with pairs
$(\ell, \{X,Y\})$ consisting of an $S$-line $\ell$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{X,Y\}$ of $\ell \cap S$.
Since an $S$-line $\ell$ admits precisely $|\ell\cap S|-1 \le \binom{|\ell \cap S|}{2}$ nontrivial linear partitions,
the claim implies that $L_S \le \binom n2 + 1$ with equality iff no three points of $S$ are collinear.

Let $P$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane. Given any nontrivial linear partition $\{A,B\}$ of $S$, the
set of lines inducing this partition is a proper, open, connected subset $I$ of $P$. (It is proper because it has to omit
directions of $S$-lines that pass through both parts of the partition and open because we can vary the separating line. It is
connected because if we have two such lines that aren't parallel, we can rotate through their point of intersection to
get all intermediate directions.) Among all $S$-lines that intersect both $A$ and $B$ choose a line $\ell$ whose direction is
minimal (in the clockwise direction) with respect to the interval $I$; also, pick an arbitrary line $\ell'$ that induces
$\{A,B\}$. By rotating $\ell'$ clockwise to $\ell$ about their point of intersection, we see that the direction
of $\ell$ is the least upper bound of $I$. (We can't hit any point of $S$ during the rotation because of the minimality
property of $\ell$.)  The line $\ell$ is in fact unique because if the (parallel) lines $pq$ and $rs$ are two choices for $\ell$,
with $p$, $q \in A$; $r$, $s \in B$, then one of the diagonals $ps$, $qr$ would contradict the minimality property of
$\ell$. To define the above bijection we send $\{A,B\}$ to $(\ell, \{A \cap \ell, B \cap \ell\})$.

Conversely, suppose that we are given an $S$-line $\ell$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{X,Y\}$ of $\ell \cap S$.
Pick any point $p \in \ell$ that induces the partition $\{X,Y\}$. If we rotate the line $\ell$ about $p$ in the counterclockwise
direction by a sufficiently small amount, we get a nontrivial linear partitition of $S$ that is independent of all choices.
(It is obtained from the partition of $S-\ell$ induced by $\ell$ by adjoining $X$ to one part and $Y$ to the other.) This
defines a map in the other direction.

By construction these two maps are inverse to each other, and this proves the claim.


\textbf{Remark:}
Given a finite set $S$ of points in $\mathbb{R}^n$, a \emph{non-Radon partition}
of $S$ is a pair $(A,B)$
of complementary subsets that can be separated by
a hyperplane. \emph{Radon's theorem} states that if $\#S\geq n+2$, then not
every $(A,B)$ is a non-Radon partition. The result of this problem
has been greatly
extended, especially within the context of matroid theory and oriented
matroid theory. Richard Stanley suggests the following references:
T. H. Brylawski, A combinatorial
perspective on the Radon convexity theorem, \emph{Geom. Ded.} \textbf{5}
(1976),
459-466; and T. Zaslavsky, Extremal arrangements of hyperplanes,
\emph{Ann. N. Y. Acad. Sci.} \textbf{440} (1985), 69-87.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $pointset$ be a finite set of points in the plane. A linear partition of $pointset$
is an unordered pair $\{subsetone,subsettwo\}$ of subsets of $pointset$ such that $subsetone \cup subsettwo = pointset$,
$subsetone \cap subsettwo = \emptyset$, and $subsetone$ and $subsettwo$ lie on opposite sides of some
straight line disjoint from $pointset$ ($subsetone$ or $subsettwo$ may be empty). Let $partitioncount$ be the
number of linear partitions of $pointset$. For each positive integer $pointcount$, find the
maximum of $partitioncount$ over all sets $pointset$ of $pointcount$ points.
descriptive_long.solution:
The maximum is $\binom{pointcount}{2} + 1$, achieved for instance by a
convex $pointcount$-gon: besides the trivial partition (in which all of the points
are in one part), each linear
partition occurs by drawing a line crossing a unique pair
of edges.

\textbf{First solution:}
We will prove that $partitioncount = \binom{pointcount}{2} + 1$ in any configuration in which
no two of the lines joining points of $pointset$ are parallel. This suffices
to imply the maximum in all configurations: given a maximal configuration,
we may vary the points slightly to get another maximal configuration in which
our hypothesis is satisfied.
For convenience, we assume $pointcount \geq 3$, as the cases $pointcount=1,2$ are easy.

Let $infinityline$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane; i.e., $infinityline$
is the set of possible directions of lines in the plane, viewed as a circle.
Remove the directions corresponding to lines through two points of $pointset$;
this leaves behind $\binom{pointcount}{2}$ intervals.

Given a direction in one of the intervals, consider the set of linear
partitions achieved by lines parallel to that direction. Note that the
resulting collection of partitions depends only on the interval. Then
note that the collections associated to adjacent intervals differ in only
one element.

The trivial partition that puts all of $pointset$ on one side is in every such
collection. We now observe that for any other linear partition
$\{subsetone,subsettwo\}$, the set of intervals to which $\{subsetone,subsettwo\}$ is:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] a consecutive block of intervals, but
\item[(b)] not all of them.
\end{enumerate}
For (a), note that if $genericline_1, genericline_2$ are nonparallel lines achieving
the same partition, then we can rotate around their point of intersection
to achieve all of the intermediate directions on one side or the other.
For (b), the case $pointcount=3$ is evident; to reduce the general case to this case,
take points $infinityline,auxpointq,auxpointr$ such that $infinityline$ lies on the opposite side of
the partition from $auxpointq$ and $auxpointr$.

It follows now that that each linear partition,
except for the trivial one, occurs in exactly one place as the partition
associated to some interval but not to its immediate counterclockwise neighbor.
In other words, the number of linear partitions is one more  than the
number of intervals, or $\binom{pointcount}{2} + 1$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the upper bound
by induction on $pointcount$. Choose a point $infinityline$ in the convex hull of $pointset$.
Put $pointset' = pointset \setminus \{infinityline\}$;
by the induction hypothesis, there are at most $\binom{pointcount-1}{2} + 1$
linear partitions of $pointset'$. Note that each linear partition of $pointset$ restricts
to a linear partition of $pointset'$. Moreover, if two linear partitions of $pointset$
restrict to the same linear partition of $pointset'$, then that partition of $pointset'$
is achieved by a line through $infinityline$.

By rotating a line through $infinityline$, we see that there are at most $pointcount-1$
partitions of $pointset'$ achieved by lines through $infinityline$: namely, the partition only
changes when the rotating line passes through one of the points of $pointset$.
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Noam Elkies) We enlarge the plane to a projective
plane by adding a line at infinity, then apply the polar duality map
centered at one of the points $centerpoint \in pointset$. This turns the rest of $pointset$ into
a set $pointset'$ of $pointcount-1$ lines in the dual projective plane. Let $centerpoint'$ be the
point in the dual plane corresponding to the original line at infinity;
it does not lie on any of the lines in $pointset'$.

Let $genericline$ be a line in the original plane, corresponding to a point $infinityline$ in
the dual plane. If we form the linear partition induced by $genericline$, then
the points of $pointset \setminus \{centerpoint\}$ lying in the same part as $centerpoint$
correspond to the lines of $pointset'$ which cross the segment $centerpoint'infinityline$.
If we consider the dual affine plane as being divided into regions by
the lines of $pointset'$, then the lines of $pointset'$ crossing the segment $centerpoint'infinityline$
are determined by which region $infinityline$ lies in.

Thus our original maximum is equal to the maximum number of regions into
which $pointcount-1$ lines divide an affine plane. By induction on $pointcount$, this number
is easily seen to be $1 + \binom{pointcount}{2}$.

\textbf{Fourth solution:} (by Florian Herzig)
Say that an \emph{$pointset$-line} is a line that intersects $pointset$ in at least two points.
We claim that the nontrivial linear partitions of $pointset$ are in natural bijection with pairs
$(genericline, \{subsetx,subsety\})$ consisting of an $pointset$-line $genericline$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{subsetx,subsety\}$ of $genericline \cap pointset$.
Since an $pointset$-line $genericline$ admits precisely $|genericline\cap pointset|-1 \le \binom{|genericline \cap pointset|}{2}$ nontrivial linear partitions,
the claim implies that $partitioncount \le \binom {pointcount}2 + 1$ with equality iff no three points of $pointset$ are collinear.

Let $infinityline$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane. Given any nontrivial linear partition $\{subsetone,subsettwo\}$ of $pointset$, the
set of lines inducing this partition is a proper, open, connected subset $I$ of $infinityline$. (It is proper because it has to omit
directions of $pointset$-lines that pass through both parts of the partition and open because we can vary the separating line. It is
connected because if we have two such lines that aren't parallel, we can rotate through their point of intersection to
get all intermediate directions.) Among all $pointset$-lines that intersect both $subsetone$ and $subsettwo$ choose a line $genericline$ whose direction is
minimal (in the clockwise direction) with respect to the interval $I$; also, pick an arbitrary line $genericline'$ that induces
$\{subsetone,subsettwo\}$. By rotating $genericline'$ clockwise to $genericline$ about their point of intersection, we see that the direction
of $genericline$ is the least upper bound of $I$. (We can't hit any point of $pointset$ during the rotation because of the minimality
property of $genericline$.)  The line $genericline$ is in fact unique because if the (parallel) lines $pointonellp pointonellq$ and $pointonellr pointonells$ are two choices for $genericline$,
with $pointonellp$, $pointonellq \in subsetone$; $pointonellr$, $pointonells \in subsettwo$, then one of the diagonals $pointonellp pointonells$, $pointonellq pointonellr$ would contradict the minimality property of
$genericline$. To define the above bijection we send $\{subsetone,subsettwo\}$ to $(genericline, \{subsetone \cap genericline, subsettwo \cap genericline\})$.

Conversely, suppose that we are given an $pointset$-line $genericline$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{subsetx,subsety\}$ of $genericline \cap pointset$.
Pick any point $pointonellp \in genericline$ that induces the partition $\{subsetx,subsety\}$. If we rotate the line $genericline$ about $pointonellp$ in the counterclockwise
direction by a sufficiently small amount, we get a nontrivial linear partitition of $pointset$ that is independent of all choices.
(It is obtained from the partition of $pointset-genericline$ induced by $genericline$ by adjoining $subsetx$ to one part and $subsety$ to the other.) This
defines a map in the other direction.

By construction these two maps are inverse to each other, and this proves the claim.


\textbf{Remark:}
Given a finite set $pointset$ of points in $\mathbb{R}^{pointcount}$, a \emph{non-Radon partition}
of $pointset$ is a pair $(subsetone,subsettwo)$
of complementary subsets that can be separated by
a hyperplane. \emph{Radon's theorem} states that if $\#pointset\geq pointcount+2$, then not
every $(subsetone,subsettwo)$ is a non-Radon partition. The result of this problem
has been greatly
extended, especially within the context of matroid theory and oriented
matroid theory. Richard Stanley suggests the following references:
T. H. Brylawski, A combinatorial
perspective on the Radon convexity theorem, \emph{Geom. Ded.} \textbf{5}
(1976),
459-466; and T. Zaslavsky, Extremal arrangements of hyperplanes,
\emph{Ann. N. Y. Acad. Sci.} \textbf{440} (1985), 69-87.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: Let $orchardset$ be a finite set of points in the plane. A linear partition of $orchardset$ is an unordered pair $\{canvasbag,lanternbox\}$ of subsets of $orchardset$ such that $canvasbag \cup lanternbox = orchardset$, $canvasbag \cap lanternbox = \emptyset$, and $canvasbag$ and $lanternbox$ lie on opposite sides of some straight line disjoint from $orchardset$ ($canvasbag$ or $lanternbox$ may be empty). Let $measurefield$ be the number of linear partitions of $orchardset$. For each positive integer $circlestep$, find the maximum of $measurefield$ over all sets $orchardset$ of $circlestep$ points.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The maximum is $\binom{circlestep}{2} + 1$, achieved for instance by a convex $circlestep$-gon: besides the trivial partition (in which all of the points are in one part), each linear partition occurs by drawing a line crossing a unique pair of edges.

\textbf{First solution:}
We will prove that $measurefield = \binom{circlestep}{2} + 1$ in any configuration in which no two of the lines joining points of $orchardset$ are parallel. This suffices to imply the maximum in all configurations: given a maximal configuration, we may vary the points slightly to get another maximal configuration in which our hypothesis is satisfied.
For convenience, we assume $circlestep \geq 3$, as the cases $circlestep=1,2$ are easy.

Let $harborpoint$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane; i.e., $harborpoint$ is the set of possible directions of lines in the plane, viewed as a circle. Remove the directions corresponding to lines through two points of $orchardset$; this leaves behind $\binom{circlestep}{2}$ intervals.

Given a direction in one of the intervals, consider the set of linear partitions achieved by lines parallel to that direction. Note that the resulting collection of partitions depends only on the interval. Then note that the collections associated to adjacent intervals differ in only one element.

The trivial partition that puts all of $orchardset$ on one side is in every such collection. We now observe that for any other linear partition $\{canvasbag,lanternbox\}$, the set of intervals to which $\{canvasbag,lanternbox\}$ is:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] a consecutive block of intervals, but
\item[(b)] not all of them.
\end{enumerate}
For (a), note that if $loopcurve_1, loopcurve_2$ are nonparallel lines achieving the same partition, then we can rotate around their point of intersection to achieve all of the intermediate directions on one side or the other.
For (b), the case $circlestep=3$ is evident; to reduce the general case to this case, take points $harborpoint,daisycurve,marblepath$ such that $harborpoint$ lies on the opposite side of the partition from $daisycurve$ and $marblepath$.

It follows now that that each linear partition, except for the trivial one, occurs in exactly one place as the partition associated to some interval but not to its immediate counterclockwise neighbor. In other words, the number of linear partitions is one more than the number of intervals, or $\binom{circlestep}{2} + 1$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the upper bound by induction on $circlestep$. Choose a point $harborpoint$ in the convex hull of $orchardset$. Put $orchardset' = orchardset \setminus \{harborpoint\}$; by the induction hypothesis, there are at most $\binom{circlestep-1}{2} + 1$ linear partitions of $orchardset'$. Note that each linear partition of $orchardset$ restricts to a linear partition of $orchardset'$. Moreover, if two linear partitions of $orchardset$ restrict to the same linear partition of $orchardset'$, then that partition of $orchardset'$ is achieved by a line through $harborpoint$.

By rotating a line through $harborpoint$, we see that there are at most $circlestep-1$ partitions of $orchardset'$ achieved by lines through $harborpoint$: namely, the partition only changes when the rotating line passes through one of the points of $orchardset$. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Noam Elkies)
We enlarge the plane to a projective plane by adding a line at infinity, then apply the polar duality map centered at one of the points $quartzline \in orchardset$. This turns the rest of $orchardset$ into a set $orchardset'$ of $circlestep-1$ lines in the dual projective plane. Let $quartzline'$ be the point in the dual plane corresponding to the original line at infinity; it does not lie on any of the lines in $orchardset'$.

Let $loopcurve$ be a line in the original plane, corresponding to a point $harborpoint$ in the dual plane. If we form the linear partition induced by $loopcurve$, then the points of $orchardset \setminus \{quartzline\}$ lying in the same part as $quartzline$ correspond to the lines of $orchardset'$ which cross the segment $quartzline'harborpoint$. If we consider the dual affine plane as being divided into regions by the lines of $orchardset'$, then the lines of $orchardset'$ crossing the segment $quartzline'harborpoint$ are determined by which region $harborpoint$ lies in.

Thus our original maximum is equal to the maximum number of regions into which $circlestep-1$ lines divide an affine plane. By induction on $circlestep$, this number is easily seen to be $1 + \binom{circlestep}{2}$.

\textbf{Fourth solution:} (by Florian Herzig)
Say that an \emph{$orchardset$-line} is a line that intersects $orchardset$ in at least two points. We claim that the nontrivial linear partitions of $orchardset$ are in natural bijection with pairs $(loopcurve, \{beaconmark,cobaltzone\})$ consisting of an $orchardset$-line $loopcurve$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{beaconmark,cobaltzone\}$ of $loopcurve \cap orchardset$. Since an $orchardset$-line $loopcurve$ admits precisely $|loopcurve\cap orchardset|-1 \le \binom{|loopcurve \cap orchardset|}{2}$ nontrivial linear partitions, the claim implies that $measurefield \le \binom{circlestep}{2} + 1$ with equality iff no three points of $orchardset$ are collinear.

Let $harborpoint$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane. Given any nontrivial linear partition $\{canvasbag,lanternbox\}$ of $orchardset$, the set of lines inducing this partition is a proper, open, connected subset $I$ of $harborpoint$. (It is proper because it has to omit directions of $orchardset$-lines that pass through both parts of the partition and open because we can vary the separating line. It is connected because if we have two such lines that aren't parallel, we can rotate through their point of intersection to get all intermediate directions.) Among all $orchardset$-lines that intersect both $canvasbag$ and $lanternbox$ choose a line $loopcurve$ whose direction is minimal (in the clockwise direction) with respect to the interval $I$; also, pick an arbitrary line $loopcurve'$ that induces $\{canvasbag,lanternbox\}$. By rotating $loopcurve'$ clockwise to $loopcurve$ about their point of intersection, we see that the direction of $loopcurve$ is the least upper bound of $I$. (We can't hit any point of $orchardset$ during the rotation because of the minimality property of $loopcurve$.)  The line $loopcurve$ is in fact unique because if the (parallel) lines $pebbletonehollowmint$ and $riverstonesapphireden$ are two choices for $loopcurve$, with $pebbletone$, $hollowmint \in canvasbag$; $riverstone$, $sapphireden \in lanternbox$, then one of the diagonals $pebbletonesapphireden$, $hollowmintriverstone$ would contradict the minimality property of $loopcurve$. To define the above bijection we send $\{canvasbag,lanternbox\}$ to $(loopcurve, \{canvasbag \cap loopcurve, lanternbox \cap loopcurve\})$.

Conversely, suppose that we are given an $orchardset$-line $loopcurve$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{beaconmark,cobaltzone\}$ of $loopcurve \cap orchardset$. Pick any point $pebbletone \in loopcurve$ that induces the partition $\{beaconmark,cobaltzone\}$. If we rotate the line $loopcurve$ about $pebbletone$ in the counterclockwise direction by a sufficiently small amount, we get a nontrivial linear partitition of $orchardset$ that is independent of all choices. (It is obtained from the partition of $orchardset-loopcurve$ induced by $loopcurve$ by adjoining $beaconmark$ to one part and $cobaltzone$ to the other.) This defines a map in the other direction.

By construction these two maps are inverse to each other, and this proves the claim.

\textbf{Remark:}
Given a finite set $orchardset$ of points in $\mathbb{R}^{circlestep}$, a \emph{non-Radon partition} of $orchardset$ is a pair $(canvasbag,lanternbox)$ of complementary subsets that can be separated by a hyperplane. \emph{Radon's theorem} states that if $\#orchardset\geq circlestep+2$, then not every $(canvasbag,lanternbox)$ is a non-Radon partition. The result of this problem has been greatly extended, especially within the context of matroid theory and oriented matroid theory. Richard Stanley suggests the following references: T. H. Brylawski, A combinatorial perspective on the Radon convexity theorem, \emph{Geom. Ded.} \textbf{5} (1976), 459-466; and T. Zaslavsky, Extremal arrangements of hyperplanes, \emph{Ann. N. Y. Acad. Sci.} \textbf{440} (1985), 69-87.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Let $voidcollect$ be a finite set of points in the plane. A linear partition of $voidcollect$ is an unordered pair $\{supercluster,ultraset\}$ of subsets of $voidcollect$ such that $supercluster \cup ultraset = voidcollect$, $supercluster \cap ultraset = \emptyset$, and $supercluster$ and $ultraset$ lie on opposite sides of some straight line disjoint from $voidcollect$ ($supercluster$ or $ultraset$ may be empty). Let $chaosdegree$ be the number of linear partitions of $voidcollect$. For each positive integer $voidcount$, find the maximum of $chaosdegree$ over all sets $voidcollect$ of $voidcount$ points.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The maximum is $\binom{voidcount}{2} + 1$, achieved for instance by a
convex $voidcount$-gon: besides the trivial partition (in which all of the points
are in one part), each linear
partition occurs by drawing a line crossing a unique pair
of edges.

We will prove that $chaosdegree = \binom{voidcount}{2} + 1$ in any configuration in which
no two of the lines joining points of $voidcollect$ are parallel. This suffices
to imply the maximum in all configurations: given a maximal configuration,
we may vary the points slightly to get another maximal configuration in which
our hypothesis is satisfied.
For convenience, we assume $voidcount \geq 3$, as the cases $voidcount=1,2$ are easy.

Let $finiteanchor$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane; i.e., $finiteanchor$
is the set of possible directions of lines in the plane, viewed as a circle.
Remove the directions corresponding to lines through two points of $voidcollect$;
this leaves behind $\binom{voidcount}{2}$ intervals.

Given a direction in one of the intervals, consider the set of linear
partitions achieved by lines parallel to that direction. Note that the
resulting collection of partitions depends only on the interval. Then
note that the collections associated to adjacent intervals differ in only
one element.

The trivial partition that puts all of $voidcollect$ on one side is in every such
collection. We now observe that for any other linear partition
$\{supercluster,ultraset\}$, the set of intervals to which $\{supercluster,ultraset\}$ is:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] a consecutive block of intervals, but
\item[(b)] not all of them.
\end{enumerate}
For (a), note that if $curvepath_1, curvepath_2$ are nonparallel lines achieving
the same partition, then we can rotate around their point of intersection
to achieve all of the intermediate directions on one side or the other.
For (b), the case $voidcount=3$ is evident; to reduce the general case to this case,
take points $finiteanchor,antipivot,nullcorner$ such that $finiteanchor$ lies on the opposite side of
the partition from $antipivot$ and $nullcorner$.

It follows now that that each linear partition,
except for the trivial one, occurs in exactly one place as the partition
associated to some interval but not to its immediate counterclockwise neighbor.
In other words, the number of linear partitions is one more  than the
number of intervals, or $\binom{voidcount}{2} + 1$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the upper bound
by induction on $voidcount$. Choose a point $finiteanchor$ in the convex hull of $voidcollect$.
Put $voidcollect' = voidcollect \setminus \{finiteanchor\}$;
by the induction hypothesis, there are at most $\binom{voidcount-1}{2} + 1$
linear partitions of $voidcollect'$. Note that each linear partition of $voidcollect$ restricts
to a linear partition of $voidcollect'$. Moreover, if two linear partitions of $voidcollect$
restrict to the same linear partition of $voidcollect'$, then that partition of $voidcollect'$
is achieved by a line through $finiteanchor$.

By rotating a line through $finiteanchor$, we see that there are at most $voidcount-1$
partitions of $voidcollect'$ achieved by lines through $finiteanchor$: namely, the partition only
changes when the rotating line passes through one of the points of $voidcollect$.
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Noam Elkies) We enlarge the plane to a projective
plane by adding a line at infinity, then apply the polar duality map
centered at one of the points $antihub \in voidcollect$. This turns the rest of $voidcollect$ into
a set $voidcollect'$ of $voidcount-1$ lines in the dual projective plane. Let $antihub'$ be the
point in the dual plane corresponding to the original line at infinity;
it does not lie on any of the lines in $voidcollect'$.

Let $curvepath$ be a line in the original plane, corresponding to a point $finiteanchor$ in
the dual plane. If we form the linear partition induced by $curvepath$, then
the points of $voidcollect \setminus \{antihub\}$ lying in the same part as $antihub$
correspond to the lines of $voidcollect'$ which cross the segment $antihub'finiteanchor$.
If we consider the dual affine plane as being divided into regions by
the lines of $voidcollect'$, then the lines of $voidcollect'$ crossing the segment $antihub'finiteanchor$
are determined by which region $finiteanchor$ lies in.

Thus our original maximum is equal to the maximum number of regions into
which $voidcount-1$ lines divide an affine plane. By induction on $voidcount$, this number
is easily seen to be $1 + \binom{voidcount}{2}$.

\textbf{Fourth solution:} (by Florian Herzig)
Say that an \emph{$voidcollect$-line} is a line that intersects $voidcollect$ in at least two points.
We claim that the nontrivial linear partitions of $voidcollect$ are in natural bijection with pairs
$(curvepath, \{outerset,innerset\})$ consisting of an $voidcollect$-line $curvepath$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{outerset,innerset\}$ of $curvepath \cap voidcollect$.
Since an $voidcollect$-line $curvepath$ admits precisely $|curvepath\cap voidcollect|-1 \le \binom{|curvepath \cap voidcollect|}{2}$ nontrivial linear partitions,
the claim implies that $chaosdegree \le \binom {voidcount}2 + 1$ with equality iff no three points of $voidcollect$ are collinear.

Let $finiteanchor$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane. Given any nontrivial linear partition $\{supercluster,ultraset\}$ of $voidcollect$, the
set of lines inducing this partition is a proper, open, connected subset $I$ of $finiteanchor$. (It is proper because it has to omit
directions of $voidcollect$-lines that pass through both parts of the partition and open because we can vary the separating line. It is
connected because if we have two such lines that aren't parallel, we can rotate through their point of intersection to
get all intermediate directions.) Among all $voidcollect$-lines that intersect both $supercluster$ and $ultraset$ choose a line $curvepath$ whose direction is
minimal (in the clockwise direction) with respect to the interval $I$; also, pick an arbitrary line $curvepath'$ that induces
$\{supercluster,ultraset\}$. By rotating $curvepath'$ clockwise to $curvepath$ about their point of intersection, we see that the direction
of $curvepath$ is the least upper bound of $I$. (We can't hit any point of $voidcollect$ during the rotation because of the minimality
property of $curvepath$.)  The line $curvepath$ is in fact unique because if the (parallel) lines $widespreadbroaddomain$ and $expansexxvastarea$ are two choices for $curvepath$,
with $widespread$, $broaddomain \in supercluster$; $expansexx$, $vastarea \in ultraset$, then one of the diagonals $widespreadvastarea$, $broaddomainexpansexx$ would contradict the minimality property of
$curvepath$. To define the above bijection we send $\{supercluster,ultraset\}$ to $(curvepath, \{supercluster \cap curvepath, ultraset \cap curvepath\})$.

Conversely, suppose that we are given an $voidcollect$-line $curvepath$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{outerset,innerset\}$ of $curvepath \cap voidcollect$.
Pick any point $widespread \in curvepath$ that induces the partition $\{outerset,innerset\}$. If we rotate the line $curvepath$ about $widespread$ in the counterclockwise
direction by a sufficiently small amount, we get a nontrivial linear partitition of $voidcollect$ that is independent of all choices.
(It is obtained from the partition of $voidcollect-curvepath$ induced by $curvepath$ by adjoining $outerset$ to one part and $innerset$ to the other.) This
defines a map in the other direction.

By construction these two maps are inverse to each other, and this proves the claim.

\textbf{Remark:}
Given a finite set $voidcollect$ of points in $\mathbb{R}^{voidcount}$, a \emph{non-Radon partition}
of $voidcollect$ is a pair $(supercluster,ultraset)$
of complementary subsets that can be separated by
a hyperplane. \emph{Radon's theorem} states that if $\#voidcollect\geq voidcount+2$, then not
every $(supercluster,ultraset)$ is a non-Radon partition. The result of this problem
has been greatly
extended, especially within the context of matroid theory and oriented
matroid theory. Richard Stanley suggests the following references:
T. H. Brylawski, A combinatorial
perspective on the Radon convexity theorem, \emph{Geom. Ded.} \textbf{5}
(1976),
459-466; and T. Zaslavsky, Extremal arrangements of hyperplanes,
\emph{Ann. N. Y. Acad. Sci.} \textbf{440} (1985), 69-87.

garbled_string.question:
Let $mczlkfth$ be a finite set of points in the plane. A linear partition of $mczlkfth$
is an unordered pair $\{grylsntk,hvdprwoq\}$ of subsets of $mczlkfth$ such that $grylsntk \cup hvdprwoq = mczlkfth$,
$grylsntk \cap hvdprwoq = \emptyset$, and $grylsntk$ and $hvdprwoq$ lie on opposite sides of some
straight line disjoint from $mczlkfth$ ($grylsntk$ or $hvdprwoq$ may be empty). Let $kzptqvne$ be the
number of linear partitions of $mczlkfth$. For each positive integer $rtwilczv$, find the
maximum of $kzptqvne$ over all sets $mczlkfth$ of $rtwilczv$ points.
garbled_string.solution:
The maximum is $\binom{rtwilczv}{2} + 1$, achieved for instance by a
convex rtwilczv-gon: besides the trivial partition (in which all of the points
are in one part), each linear
partition occurs by drawing a line crossing a unique pair
of edges.

\textbf{First solution:}
We will prove that $kzptqvne = \binom{rtwilczv}{2} + 1$ in any configuration in which
no two of the lines joining points of $mczlkfth$ are parallel. This suffices
to imply the maximum in all configurations: given a maximal configuration,
we may vary the points slightly to get another maximal configuration in which
our hypothesis is satisfied.
For convenience, we assume $rtwilczv \geq 3$, as the cases $rtwilczv=1,2$ are easy.

Let $dhtuojcq$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane; i.e., $dhtuojcq$
is the set of possible directions of lines in the plane, viewed as a circle.
Remove the directions corresponding to lines through two points of $mczlkfth$;
this leaves behind $\binom{rtwilczv}{2}$ intervals.

Given a direction in one of the intervals, consider the set of linear
partitions achieved by lines parallel to that direction. Note that the
resulting collection of partitions depends only on the interval. Then
note that the collections associated to adjacent intervals differ in only
one element.

The trivial partition that puts all of $mczlkfth$ on one side is in every such
collection. We now observe that for any other linear partition
$\{grylsntk,hvdprwoq\}$, the set of intervals to which $\{grylsntk,hvdprwoq\}$ is:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] a consecutive block of intervals, but
\item[(b)] not all of them.
\end{enumerate}
For (a), note that if $afkjdwju_1, afkjdwju_2$ are nonparallel lines achieving
the same partition, then we can rotate around their point of intersection
to achieve all of the intermediate directions on one side or the other.
For (b), the case $rtwilczv=3$ is evident; to reduce the general case to this case,
take points $dhtuojcq,smvrekjh,qplwznie$ such that $dhtuojcq$ lies on the opposite side of
the partition from $smvrekjh$ and $qplwznie$.

It follows now that each linear partition,
except for the trivial one, occurs in exactly one place as the partition
associated to some interval but not to its immediate counterclockwise neighbor.
In other words, the number of linear partitions is one more than the
number of intervals, or $\binom{rtwilczv}{2} + 1$ as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the upper bound
by induction on $rtwilczv$. Choose a point $dhtuojcq$ in the convex hull of $mczlkfth$.
Put $mczlkfth' = mczlkfth \setminus \{dhtuojcq\}$;
by the induction hypothesis, there are at most $\binom{rtwilczv-1}{2} + 1$
linear partitions of $mczlkfth'$. Note that each linear partition of $mczlkfth$ restricts
to a linear partition of $mczlkfth'$. Moreover, if two linear partitions of $mczlkfth$
restrict to the same linear partition of $mczlkfth'$, then that partition of $mczlkfth'$
is achieved by a line through $dhtuojcq$.

By rotating a line through $dhtuojcq$, we see that there are at most $rtwilczv-1$
partitions of $mczlkfth'$ achieved by lines through $dhtuojcq$: namely, the partition only
changes when the rotating line passes through one of the points of $mczlkfth$.
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Noam Elkies) We enlarge the plane to a projective
plane by adding a line at infinity, then apply the polar duality map
centered at one of the points $wykjrbox \in mczlkfth$. This turns the rest of $mczlkfth$ into
a set $mczlkfth'$ of $rtwilczv-1$ lines in the dual projective plane. Let $wykjrbox'$ be the
point in the dual plane corresponding to the original line at infinity;
it does not lie on any of the lines in $mczlkfth'$.

Let $afkjdwju$ be a line in the original plane, corresponding to a point $dhtuojcq$ in
the dual plane. If we form the linear partition induced by $afkjdwju$, then
the points of $mczlkfth \setminus \{wykjrbox\}$ lying in the same part as $wykjrbox$
correspond to the lines of $mczlkfth'$ which cross the segment $wykjrbox'dhtuojcq$.
If we consider the dual affine plane as being divided into regions by
the lines of $mczlkfth'$, then the lines of $mczlkfth'$ crossing the segment $wykjrbox'dhtuojcq$
are determined by which region $dhtuojcq$ lies in.

Thus our original maximum is equal to the maximum number of regions into
which $rtwilczv-1$ lines divide an affine plane. By induction on $rtwilczv$, this number
is easily seen to be $1 + \binom{rtwilczv}{2}$.

\textbf{Fourth solution:} (by Florian Herzig)
Say that an \emph{$mczlkfth$-line} is a line that intersects $mczlkfth$ in at least two points.
We claim that the nontrivial linear partitions of $mczlkfth$ are in natural bijection with pairs
$(afkjdwju, \{fzygklum,ncrhawod\})$ consisting of an $mczlkfth$-line $afkjdwju$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{fzygklum,ncrhawod\}$ of $afkjdwju \cap mczlkfth$.
Since an $mczlkfth$-line $afkjdwju$ admits precisely $|afkjdwju\cap mczlkfth|-1 \le \binom{|afkjdwju \cap mczlkfth|}{2}$ nontrivial linear partitions,
the claim implies that $kzptqvne \le \binom {rtwilczv}2 + 1$ with equality iff no three points of $mczlkfth$ are collinear.

Let $dhtuojcq$ be the line at infinity in the real projective plane. Given any nontrivial linear partition $\{grylsntk,hvdprwoq\}$ of $mczlkfth$, the
set of lines inducing this partition is a proper, open, connected subset $I$ of $dhtuojcq$. (It is proper because it has to omit
directions of $mczlkfth$-lines that pass through both parts of the partition and open because we can vary the separating line. It is
connected because if we have two such lines that aren't parallel, we can rotate through their point of intersection to
get all intermediate directions.) Among all $mczlkfth$-lines that intersect both $grylsntk$ and $hvdprwoq$ choose a line $afkjdwju$ whose direction is
minimal (in the clockwise direction) with respect to the interval $I$; also, pick an arbitrary line $afkjdwju'$ that induces
$\{grylsntk,hvdprwoq\}$. By rotating $afkjdwju'$ clockwise to $afkjdwju$ about their point of intersection, we see that the direction
of $afkjdwju$ is the least upper bound of $I$. (We can't hit any point of $mczlkfth$ during the rotation because of the minimality
property of $afkjdwju$.)  The line $afkjdwju$ is in fact unique because if the (parallel) lines $xqdeinvfbmtylcra$ and $hjskveorvdzuqmnl$ are two choices for $afkjdwju$,
with $xqdeinvf$, $bmtylcra \in grylsntk$; $hjskveor$, $vdzuqmnl \in hvdprwoq$, then one of the diagonals $xqdeinvfvdzuqmnl$, $bmtylcrahjskveor$ would contradict the minimality property of
$afkjdwju$. To define the above bijection we send $\{grylsntk,hvdprwoq\}$ to $(afkjdwju, \{grylsntk \cap afkjdwju, hvdprwoq \cap afkjdwju\})$.

Conversely, suppose that we are given an $mczlkfth$-line $afkjdwju$ and a nontrivial linear partition $\{fzygklum,ncrhawod\}$ of $afkjdwju \cap mczlkfth$.
Pick any point $xqdeinvf \in afkjdwju$ that induces the partition $\{fzygklum,ncrhawod\}$. If we rotate the line $afkjdwju$ about $xqdeinvf$ in the counterclockwise
direction by a sufficiently small amount, we get a nontrivial linear partitition of $mczlkfth$ that is independent of all choices.
(It is obtained from the partition of $mczlkfth-afkjdwju$ induced by $afkjdwju$ by adjoining $fzygklum$ to one part and $ncrhawod$ to the other.) This
defines a map in the other direction.

By construction these two maps are inverse to each other, and this proves the claim.


\textbf{Remark:}
Given a finite set $mczlkfth$ of points in $\mathbb{R}^{rtwilczv}$, a \emph{non-Radon partition}
of $mczlkfth$ is a pair $(grylsntk,hvdprwoq)$
of complementary subsets that can be separated by
a hyperplane. \emph{Radon's theorem} states that if $\#mczlkfth\geq rtwilczv+2$, then not
every $(grylsntk,hvdprwoq)$ is a non-Radon partition. The result of this problem
has been greatly
extended, especially within the context of matroid theory and oriented
matroid theory. Richard Stanley suggests the following references:
T. H. Brylawski, A combinatorial
perspective on the Radon convexity theorem, \emph{Geom. Ded.} \textbf{5}
(1976),
459-466; and T. Zaslavsky, Extremal arrangements of hyperplanes,
\emph{Ann. N. Y. Acad. Sci.} \textbf{440} (1985), 69-87.

kernel_variant.question:
Let S be a finite set of n \geq  1 distinct points in the Euclidean plane.  

An oriented linear separation of S is an ordered pair (A , B) of (possibly empty) disjoint subsets with A \cup  B = S for which there exists an oriented straight line \ell  that is disjoint from S and whose open left half-plane contains A while the open right half-plane contains B.  (Reversing the orientation of \ell  exchanges the two parts.)  Denote by O_S the number of oriented linear separations of S.

For every positive integer n define
  M(n) = max{ O_S : |S| = n } .

Determine the value of M(n) and exhibit, for each n, a configuration of n points for which the maximum is attained.
kernel_variant.solution:
Answer.
For every integer n \geq  1
  M(n) = 2\cdot C(n , 2) + 2 = n (n - 1) + 2 .
The maximum is attained
* for n = 1 by any single point,
* for n = 2 by any two distinct points, and
* for n \geq  3 by the set of vertices of a strictly convex n-gon.


1.  From oriented to unordered separations

Let L_S denote the number of (unordered) linear partitions {A , B} of S, i.e. partitions realised by some line disjoint from S.  Each unordered partition gives rise to exactly two oriented separations (choose which part goes to the left), whence
  O_S = 2 \cdot  L_S.  (1)
Consequently
  M(n) = 2 \cdot  max{ L_S : |S| = n }.  (2)
Thus it suffices to show the sharp upper bound
  L_S \leq  C(n , 2) + 1  (3)
and to exhibit configurations that attain it.


2.  Universal upper bound for L_S

Proposition.  For every finite set S of n \geq  1 points in the plane
  L_S \leq  C(n , 2) + 1,
and this bound is tight.

Proof by induction on n.
Base step n = 1.  Any partition must be {S,\emptyset }, so L_S = 1 = C(1 , 2)+1.

Base step n = 2.  With two points p,q there are exactly two linear partitions:
 * the trivial one { {p,q},\emptyset  }, and
 * the non-trivial one { {p},{q} } obtained by any line parallel to pq and separating the two points.
Thus L_S = 2 = C(2 , 2)+1, establishing the base.

Induction step.  Assume n \geq  3 and that (3) holds for all smaller sizes.  Pick a vertex P of the convex hull of S and put S' = S \ {P}.  Every linear partition of S is of exactly one of the following types.

a)  The supporting line avoids P.  Deleting P yields a partition of S'; this operation is injective, so there are at most L_{S'} such partitions.

b)  The supporting line passes through P.  While a line rotates half-way about P the induced partition changes only when the line meets another point of S; there are n-1 such directions, so at most n-1 distinct partitions of type (b) occur.

Therefore
  L_S \leq  L_{S'} + (n - 1).  (4)
By the induction hypothesis
  L_{S'} \leq  C(n-1 , 2) + 1 = (n-1)(n-2)/2 + 1.
Substituting this into (4) gives
  L_S \leq  (n-1)(n-2)/2 + 1 + (n-1)
     = (n-1)(n-2 + 2)/2 + 1 = (n-1)n/2 + 1 = C(n , 2) + 1.
This completes the induction.

Tightness.  If S is in strictly convex position (no three points collinear) then every unordered pair of edges of its convex hull is crossed by some line meeting those two edges and no vertex; different pairs produce different partitions.  Counting the single trivial partition as well, we obtain exactly C(n , 2) + 1 partitions, so the bound is tight.
\blacksquare 


3.  The maximum number of oriented separations

From (1) and the proposition we get
  O_S = 2\cdot L_S \leq  2\cdot (C(n , 2)+1) = n(n-1)+2.
Taking the maximum over all sets of n points yields
  M(n) \leq  n(n-1)+2.  (5)


4.  Configurations that attain the bound

* n = 1.  With S = {P} one has L_S = 1, hence O_S = 2, matching (5).

* n = 2.  With any two distinct points {p,q} we found L_S = 2, so O_S = 4, again matching (5).

* n \geq  3.  Let S be the vertex set of a strictly convex n-gon V_1V_2\ldots V_n.  Choose two distinct edges V_iV_{i+1} and V_jV_{j+1} (indices modulo n, 1 \leq  i < j \leq  n).  Any line \ell  crossing exactly those two edges separates the vertices into
  {V_{i+1},\ldots ,V_j} and S \ {V_{i+1},\ldots ,V_j}.
Because the pair of edges was unordered, each unordered pair yields two oriented separations (depending on which side of \ell  is declared the left half-plane).  Since there are C(n , 2) unordered edge pairs, we obtain 2\cdot C(n , 2) non-trivial oriented separations, and adding the two trivial ones (S,\emptyset ) and (\emptyset ,S) gives
  O_S = 2\cdot C(n , 2) + 2 = n(n-1)+2.
Equality in (5) is therefore achieved, so
  M(n) = n(n-1)+2 for all n \geq  1.


5.  Summary

(1)  Each unordered partition contributes two oriented separations.
(2)  Induction shows L_S \leq  C(n , 2)+1, with equality when S is strictly convex (or when n \leq  2 by direct inspection).
(3)  Hence O_S \leq  n(n-1)+2.  The bound is attained by a single point (n = 1), by any two points (n = 2), and by a strictly convex n-gon (n \geq  3).
Therefore M(n) = n(n - 1) + 2 for every positive integer n.

core_steps: Put points in generic position so that all n·(n−1)/2 connecting lines have distinct directions
Identify directions with a circle (projective line at infinity) and delete the binom(n,2) directions determined by S-lines, leaving that many open intervals

For a fixed interval, all separating lines with direction in it yield the same collection of partitions; adjacent intervals differ by exactly one non-trivial partition, and the trivial partition appears in every interval
Any non-trivial partition is realized in precisely one ‘difference’ between two consecutive interval-collections, giving a bijection between such partitions and the intervals
Hence total partitions = #intervals + 1 = binom(n,2)+1, which is attainable (e.g. convex n-gon)

slot1.description: Generic-position hypothesis needed only to ensure that the set of forbidden directions is of size exactly binom(n,2); any perturbation that keeps directions pairwise distinct works equally well.
slot1.original: “No two of the lines joining points of S are parallel.”

slot2.description: Choice of parameterising directions and of the orientation used to speak of ‘adjacent’ or ‘clockwise-neighbor’ intervals has no impact on the argument.
slot2.original: Use of the projective ‘line at infinity’ viewed as a circle with a fixed clockwise orientation.
